items.correct: Art that does not depict any recognisable object - exploring form, colour, and line directly.
items.misconception: Art that looks 'a bit like' something but not exactly.
items.random_wrong: Art that doesn't represent any country.
items.why: Students soften the term to 'stylised'. Non-representational means NO reference to objects at all - pure visual exploration (Rothko, Mondrian), distinct from semi-abstract work that simplifies recognisable forms.

items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.
It's 1996. “1986” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Beeple's record NFT sale at Christie's was in:
items.why: Correct answer: 2021. “2020” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Beeple's Everydays sold at Christie's for approximately how many million US dollars?
items.why: The real figure is 69. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
